mnemonics.title: CAP Theorem
mnemonics.content: CAP: Remember as 'Choose Any Two' - Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance cannot all be guaranteed simultaneously during network partition.

mnemonics.title: Key-Value Store Advantages
mnemonics.content: SHE L P: Scalable, High-performance, Easy-partition, Low-latency, Simple-model, Flexible-schema

acronyms.usage: Refers to non-relational databases that provide flexible schemas and horizontal scalability.
A theorem describing tradeoffs in distributed database systems.
A consistency model prioritizing system availability during network partitions.
A consistency model prioritizing consistency during network partitions.
Traditional database using tables, rows, and SQL queries.
Multiple virtual points on a hash ring representing physical nodes for better load distribution.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a circular ring where keys and nodes are placed. Keys map to the next node clockwise. When adding/removing nodes, only a small portion of keys need to remap.

Think of a large bookshelf divided into multiple smaller shelves (shards), each stored in different rooms. To find a book (value), you first find which shelf (shard) it belongs to using the book ID (key).

analogies.analogy: Like a dictionary (book, not data structure). You look up a word (key) to find its definition (value). You cannot search definitions, only look up by exact word.
Like adding more lanes to a highway rather than making one lane wider (vertical scaling). More parallel paths handle more traffic.
Like posting a status on social media. Your followers may see it at slightly different times, but eventually everyone sees the same update.
Like dividing a large book series into volumes. Each volume contains a subset of chapters, and you need to know which volume to look in to find a specific chapter.
